summary: DHH celebrates Ruby on Rails' rapid community growth just six weeks after launch, highlighting how developers from two distinct backgrounds are flocking to the framework. He shares testimonials from PHP/Perl developers who felt trapped writing unmaintainable code and found liberation in Rails' elegance. He also notes disillusioned Java/J2EE developers moonlighting with Rails in their spare time. The post frames Rails as an antidote to the 'mental agony' of messy codebases, connecting code elegance to developer happiness and self-esteem.
key_points: Rails attracted two distinct developer groups: dynamic language users (PHP, Perl, Python) struggling with maintainability, and Java/J2EE developers disillusioned with enterprise tooling
Developer happiness and self-esteem are closely tied to the perceived elegance of their code — messy codebases cause genuine mental distress
The 'broken window' metaphor: once code quality degrades past a threshold, developers see no option but to tear everything down and start over
Rails community formed remarkably fast, driven by sincere enthusiasm rather than corporate mandate
money_quotes: The mental agony of creating "a mess" lies deep with most developers.
The elegance with which we've solved the challenge is closely correlated with feelings of accomplishment and even happiness.
It can very easily be straight on depressing to work without these positive feelings.
And that's when a single broken window becomes a ghetto and you can see no way out by to tear it all down and try again.
So to be able to drag yourself out of that spot and into at least the perception of elegance can have a very positive effect on your self-esteem and -image.
key_insight: Rails succeeded not primarily on technical merits but because it restored the emotional connection between developers and their craft, replacing drudgery with joy.
